Japanese curry omelette with beef patty

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for omelette curry with hamburger steak. FOODON_00002372 (curry dish) used as the closest broader category anchor. The dish combines a Japanese-style hamburger steak (hanbāgu), a soft egg omelette, and Japanese curry sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (meat-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible broader anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for omelette curry with hamburger steak.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Japanese dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of omelette curry with hamburger steak is approximately 380 g, comprising a hamburger patty (~120 g), a soft egg omelette (~80 g), Japanese curry sauce (~130 g), and a small portion of rice or accompaniment (~50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Omelette Curry With Hamburger Steak — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Omelette Curry With Hamburger Steak (cr_id G00010934). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (beef patty + egg + curry sauce); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for omelette curry with hamburger steak. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese curry with rice/flour-thickened sauce has a GI analogous to white rice-based curry dishes (GI ~60–70 per University of Sydney GI Database). The hamburger patty and egg components have negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 380 g serving = 112.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 73.0 g; however, the dish is typically consumed as a mixed plate where the rice/carbohydrate portion is approximately 70 g available carbohydrate, yielding GL = 65 × 70 / 100 = 45.5, rounded to 45.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: At 487 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 380 g serving delivers approximately 1,850 mg sodium — over 80% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — and the potassium level of 197 mg/100g falls just below the 200 mg/100g threshold needed to partially offset sodium load, making this dish a high concern for individuals managing hypertension; request reduced-salt preparation and pair with high-potassium, high-fibre sides such as steamed broccoli or spinach.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.6 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) and moderate sugar (5.95 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of 29.6 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g, dietary fibre of only 1.8 g/100g (well below the 3 g/100g threshold), and an estimated medium GI of 65 creates a high glycaemic load per serving that is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholegrain breadcrumbs in the patty, reduced-sugar curry sauce, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ground beef and egg, combined with high sodium and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground beef in the hamburger patty contributes saturated fat (estimated ~3.5 g/100g) and cholesterol (29 mg/100g), and the high sodium content of 487 mg/100g raises cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request leaner ground meat or a chicken/turkey patty substitute, and choose a lower-sodium curry preparation to reduce combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (ground beef/pork) high in arachidonic acid; pan-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; tomato is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground beef or pork in the hamburger patty is a primary pro-inflammatory food for rheumatoid arthritis due to high arachidonic acid (omega-6) content; pan-frying further generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that amplify joint inflammation, and tomato in the curry sauce is a nightshade that may trigger individual flares — substituting ground chicken or turkey, requesting baked or grilled preparation, and monitoring nightshade tolerance is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Omelette Curry with Hamburger Steak (オムレツカレーハンバーグ, Omuretsu Karē Hanbāgu) is a Japanese-origin Western-style comfort dish popular in Singapore's Japanese casual dining and family restaurant (famiresu) culture. The dish consists of three components: a pan-fried hamburger steak (hanbāgu) made from seasoned ground beef mixed with breadcrumbs, egg, and onion; a soft, lightly folded egg omelette draped over or alongside the patty; and a rich Japanese-style curry sauce made from curry roux, tomato, onion, and stock, poured generously over the entire plate. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and slightly sweet from the curry sauce, with a tender, juicy patty and a silky egg texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, 29.57 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 487 mg sodium. The high sodium content (487 mg/100g) makes it a concern for hypertension management. The refined carbohydrate load combined with low fibre (1.8 g/100g) and an estimated medium glycaemic index of 65 creates a high glycaemic load per serving, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without modification. The red meat content raises concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is typically served as a lunch or dinner main course and is widely available in Japanese family restaurants and food courts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010934, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. All other fields are derived from approved sources or defensible analogy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB source record; value is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for a mixed dish of ground beef patty, egg omelette, and curry sauce (USDA SR Legacy entries for ground beef 80% lean, whole egg, and curry sauce). This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese curry sauce with flour-thickened roux as the dominant carbohydrate source, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database framework for curry-based rice dishes (GI range 60–70). 4. The dish is not certified halal; pork may be present in some hamburger patty preparations. 5. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
